text: ARIGAL, a mountain of Ireland, in co. Donegal, 3 m. W of the Derryveagh range. Alt. 2,462 ft. I. ARI
ARIGAS, a town on the coast of Sumatra, in the territory of Acheen, in N lat. 3° 39', E long. 96° 19'.

ARIGNA, a district in co. Roscommon, lying along a rivulet of the same name, an affluent of the Shannon, 84 m. NW of Dublin. It is an important mining district, having above 20,000 acres of coal, and a good field of iron-ore.
ARIGNANO, a town of Tuscany, between Arezzo and Florence, on the r. bank of the Arno.
ARIGUANATUBA, a large island in the Amazon, in S lat. 1° 27', one of those which divide the Coqueta into two branches.
ARIGUANCA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Cuzco, on the Oropesa.
ARIGUAS, a district of Guayana, intendancy of Nueva, in N lat. 7°.
ARIJA, a village of Sinde, on a fertile island formed by the Indus and its offset the Narra, in N lat. 27° 22'.
ARIMA, a town of Japan, in a district of the same name, in N lat. 31° 45'.
ARIMATSI, a village of Japan, in the island of Nifon. Cotton stuffs are manufactured here.
ARIMNABA, an island in the gulf of Paria, in N lat. 10° 9½', W long. 62°.
ARIMOA, an island off the E coast of Timor, in S lat. 8° 20', E long. 127° 4'. It is said to be fertile and populous.

ARINOS, a river of Brazil, rising in the mountains of Parexis, and uniting with the Tapajos. It has a W branch called Rio Negro, and the Sumiduro flows into it on the S. The mines of Santa Isabel, discovered near its source in 1747, were soon abandoned, not proving remunerative.
ARINTHOD, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura. The cant. comprises 26 com., with a pop. of 10,641. The com., which is 21 m. S of Lons-le-Saulnier, has a pop. of 1,730.
ARIO, a town of Mexico, in the dep. of Valladolid, at the foot of the Jorullo.
ARIOLA, a town of Naples, in Principato Ultra, 14 m. WSW of Benevento.
ARIOWARA, a town of Swedish Lapland, in N lat. 69° 30′, E long. 25° 34′.

ARIPO, or ARIPPO, a village and fort of Ceylon, on the gulf of Manar, 120 m. N of Columbo, in N lat. 8° 42', E long. 79° 47'. It lies opposite the celebrated pearl-oyster banks, which attract a large concourse of people, consisting of divers, boat-owners, and speculators, in the months of March and April. The pearl-banks are from 6 to 8 m. distance from the coast, in from 5½ to 7 fath. They are protected on the W and SW by a ridge of sand and coral.
ARIPUANA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, with a settlement on its banks of the same name. It joins the Madeira, after a course of about 150 m.
ARISAIG, a promontory on the W coast of Inverness-shire, in the district of Glenelg.—Also a settle ment in Sydney county, in Nova Scotia, a few miles to the W of Antigonishe bay.
ARISALA, a town of Hindostan, in the Mysore, 40 m. E of Chinna Balabarum.

ARISCH (EL), a town and fort of Syria, or of Egypt rather, on a gulf of the Mediterranean to which it gives name, in N lat. 31° 5½, E long. 33° 48′ 10″, 36 m. SW of Gaza, and 120 NE of Suez. It is situated on a slight eminence of shell-limestone rock, about half-a-mile from the sea, from which it is hidden by sand-hills and clumps of palms. The principal part is enclosed within a high wall of considerable thickness, with loopholes for musketry. It has also a large square castle, and is garrisoned by some 60 or 80 soldiers of the pasha. It is the Rhinocorura or Rhinocolura of the Greeks and Romans, and has always, since the days of the first Ptolemy, been in the hands of the Egyptian rulers, as their frontier-town on the Syrian side. The French became masters of and fortified it in 1799, but it was retaken by the Turks and English at the end of the year. In 1800, the Turks and French signed a convention here, by which the troops of the latter were to evacuate Egypt; but the English admiral refused to ratify Sir Sydney Smith's capitulation. The district of El Arisch is supposed to contain 2,000 inhabitants.—Thevenot.— Irby and Mangles.—Nugent.—Richardson.
ARISDONG, a town of Tibet, in N lat. 29° 49', E long. 84° 46'.
ARISHKERD, a plain of Kurdistan, extending from Diyadin to beyond Molla Suleiman, a distance of 40 m., with a breadth varying from 6 to 16 m. It contains about 30 villages.—Brant.
ARISPE, a town of Mexico, the cap. of the intendancy of Sonora. It is situated near the head of the Yaqui, in N lat. 31°, W long. 111°. Pop. 7,000.
ARITZAN, a town of Turkey, in Bulgaria, 10 m. S of Viddin.
ARIUI, a river of Cumana, which falls into the Orinoco, after a course of about 120 m.
ARIZA, a town of Aragon, 14 m. W of Calatayud. It carries on some trade in wine and saffron.
ARIZZU, a village of Sardinia, 32 m. E of Oristano. A coarse sort of serge, and knitted caps and hose are manufactured here. Pop. 1,821.
ARJEPLOY, a parish in Swedish Lapland, near the lake of Hornawan, in N lat. 65° 57'. Silver was at one time mined in this district.
ARJONA, a town of Andalusia, 19 m. NW from Jaen, near the Salado. It has a manufactory of china-ware. Pop. 3,200.
ARJUZANX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Landes, 15 m. N of Tartas. Pop. 650.
ARK, an island in the South Pacific, off the S coast of Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 43° 17', E long. 147° 19'.
ARKA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, 21 m. W of Malatia.—Also a town of Siberia, on the banks of the river Arka, a tributary of the Okhota, 66 m. from Okhotsk.
ARKADINSKAIA, a town of Russian Tartary, 124 m. SW of Saratov, on the Medveditza.
ARKA-IRA, a country of Siberia, on the E bank of the Petshora, on the shores of the Arctic ocean, inhabited by Samoyedes.

ARKANSAS, one of the western states of the North American confederacy; bounded on the N by the state of Missouri; E by the Mississippi, which separates it from Tennessee and Mississippi; S by Louisiana and Texas; and W by Texas. It lies between lat. 33° and 36° 30' N, and long. 89° 30' and 94° 30′ W. It is 240 m. long, and 228 m. in average breadth. Area 54,500 sq. m., or 34,880,000 acres. Pop. in 1810, 1,062; in 1830, 30,388; in 1835, 58,134, of whom 10,000 were slaves; in 1840, 97,574, of whom 19,935 were slaves; in 1845, 145,000. The number of counties, in 1820, was 7; in 1828, 15; in 1840, 40, viz.:— Counties. Pop. in 1840. Pop. in 1840. Counties. Arkansas, Desha, Poinsett Franklin, Union, Bradley, (erected since census.) Little Rock, on the S bank of the Arkansas, is the seat of government. The Arkansas flows through the central part from NW to SE; the Mississippi forms the eastern, and Red river a part of the southern boundary. The other principal rivers are the White river, whose waters are divided from the basin of the Arkansas by the Black mountains; the St. Francis, and the Washita or Ouachitta rivers. The latter river flows nearly parallel with the Arkansas and the Red river. The Black mountains, or Ozark-a provincial corruption of Arkansas, or, according to some, of Osage-traverse the country from NE to SW; and a ridge called the Masserne mountains branches off from the Ozark, eastwards to the south of the Missouri. The mountains are chiefly composed of secondary rocks, limestones, clay-slates, and sandstones. Near the SW part of the state is a singular detached elevation called Mount Prairie. The country between this range and the Mississippi is, in general, low and level, and, in many places, liable to annual inundation. To the NW of the mountains, it consists mostly of extensive prairies, without trees, except on the borders of the streams. The soil, on the rivers, especially on the Washita and the St. Francis, is exceedingly fertile; but in other parts much of it is sterile. Throughout most of the country, there is great scarcity of water. The climate is subject to great extremes of heat and cold, and is unhealthy to new settlers. Heavy rains and thunder occur sometimes daily for a period of 30 or 36 days in succession. At other times long droughts are experienced. Much of the country being a dead level, especially along the Arkansas, great tracts appear covered with stagnant bayous or pools; and the air in the thinly timbered bottoms is always close and unhealthy. Cotton and Indian corn are the staple productions. The former becomes an uncertain crop N of the St. Francis. The cotton often commands the best price in the British market. The agricultural produce of the state in 1840 was 6,028,642 lbs. of cotton; 148,439 of tobacco; 1,542 of sugar; 64,943 of wool; 105,878 bushels of wheat; 4,846,632 of Indian corn; 189,553 of oats; 293,608 of potatoes. The products of the forest were valued at 176,617 dollars; of the dairy, 59,205 dollars; of the orchard, 10,680 dollars.—The amount of home-made manufactures was 489,750 dollars. The amount of cap. employed in manufactures, 424,467 dollars. At the close of 1840, the state debt was 3,755,362 dollars; and, unfortunately for its creditors, A. is one of the states which has repudiated its public debts. The governor is elected once in 4 years. He may veto bills; but a majority of the members of each house may pass them notwithstanding. Every white male citizen of the United States, 21 years old, and who has resided in A. for the six months next before the election, may vote in the county where he resides. Representatives must be 25 years old; senators, 30. Representatives are chosen biennially; senators, for 4 years. In 1847 it should hereafter be incorporated or established in this state! The country is exceedingly well-adapted the thick forests. Limestone, gypsum, and stonecoal are abundant on the White river; and iron ore springs of the Washita are in N lat. 34° 31', W long. 95° 50'. Their highest temperature is 154°. They are much resorted to by invalids, and are useful in chronic and paralytic affections. The temperature, in the driest seasons, is at the boiling point, but is subject to considerable variation. - The principal tribes of Indians in this territory are the Osages, Cherokees, Quapaus, Choctaws, Cadoes, and Camanches. -Arkansas was discovered and settled by the French, under the chevalier De Tonti, as early as 1685. In the various transfers of territory, it followed the fate of other parts of Louisiana, until 1819, when that part of Louisiana between the state of Louisiana, or N lat. 33°, and the southern boundary of Missouri, was erected into a separate territorial government; and in 1836 it was admitted into the Union as an independent state.

ARKANSAS, a river of N America, next to the Missouri the largest affluent of the Mississippi, and formerly denominated the Great River of Louisiana. It rises in about W long. 112°, N lat. 41° 45′, in the great range of mountains which stretches in a SE and NW direction along the frontiers of New Mexico; and which, from the glistening of the during snows on their frozen sides and summits, under the reflection of the solar rays, have received the appellation of the Shining mountains. One ridge of this chain separates the source of the A. from that of Colter's river, one of the head-waters of the Rio del Norte; and by another ridge, both its source and the uppermost part of its course are separated from those of the Platte. From its source to its mouth, the A. has a very winding course of 2,173 m. in a SE direction, and 1,500 m. in a direct line. Of this course 192 m. are performed among the mountains, and 1,981 m. through the plains of Upper Louisiana. During its mountainous course, it is alternately bounded by perpendicular precipices, immense cliffs, or small prairies, on which the buffalo and elk have found means to arrive, and are almost secure from their destroyer, man. In many places the river precipitates itself over rocks, so as to be visible one moment only in the boiling and foaming of its waters, at the next disappearing in the chasms of the overhanging precipices. The A. is a large, deep, and navigable stream, for several hundred miles below its source, being fed with numerous streams from the mountains; but there is one singular circumstance attending it, namely, that, at the distance of 400 m. from its source, and 200 m. after leaving the mountains, the bed of the river becomes extremely wide, and a perfect sand-bar for several hundred miles below, as far as the mouth of the Negracka, in the summer-season; the water then standing in ponds, there not being a sufficiency to procure a running course all that distance. There seems no other way of accounting for this phenomenon but on the supposition that the low, dry, and sandy soil of Louisiana imbibes all the waters which flow from the hills and mountains, and renders the river less navigable in the dry season, at the distance of 500 than at 200 m. from its source. The same circumstance is peculiar to all rivers whose courses lie through warm, dry, and sandy countries, whose waters are more or less imbibed by the thirsty soil, or absorbed by the intense heat of the solar rays. The A. receives a number of large tributary streams in the latter half of its course, as the Negracka or Red Fork, the Neskalouska, the Nesuketonga, the Grand Canadian river, and the Pottoe, from the S; and the Vermilion, Grand, Des Illinois, and Au Mellieu rivers from the N. Of these, the three first run separate courses of 200 m. each; but the Canadian river is so large as to be reputed the main branch of the A., running 1,000 m. before it joins the main stream. The annual flooding of the A. commences early in March, I ARL and attains its greatest height in the delta of Louisiana in the month of June. In the spring-floods steam-boats can ascend it nearly to the Ozark mountains; at other seasons it is navigable a distance of nearly 300 m. to Little Rock.

ARKANSAS, a co. in the state of the same name, on both sides of the Arkansas river, near its mouth. The S end of Grand Prairie is in this co. Its surface is in general level. Pop. 1,346. Its cap., of the same name, is on the N bank of the A. river, in N lat. 34°, 50 m. above the Mississippi.
ARKAVEH, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Trebisond, on the Black sea, 35 m. NE of Rizeh.
ARKAVIA, a town of Wallachia, 18 m. SE of Hermannstadt, at the foot of the Magura mountains.
ARKEL, a district and village of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland, on the river Waal, 3 m. N of Gorcum.

ARKENGARTHDALE, a parish and town in the N. R. of Yorkshire, on the Swale, 10 m. from Richmond. Area 14,180 acres. Pop. 1,243. It contains valuable lead-mines which were worked in the reign of King John, and which still produce between 2,000 and 3,000 tons of lead annually.
ARKESDEN, a parish in Essex, 5 m. S of Saffron- Walden. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 498.
ARKHOLME, a chapelry and village in Lancashire, in the p. of Melling, 5 m. SSW of Kirby-Lonsdale. Area 2,920 acres. Pop. with Cawood 407.

ARKI'KO', or ARKEEKO, a seaport at the head of Massowah bay, in Abyssinia, in N lat. 15° 34′ 45″, E long. 39° 37′ 15". Various travellers have described it as a mere collection of miserable huts, built of clay and grass, and occupied by a barbarous race, the scourge and dread even of their savage neighbours. The harbour, however, formed by a ring of rocks, is extensive and safe, deficient only in one point forming the entrance. The inhabitants trade with the Arabian coast in corn and slaves.
ARKITKAN, a town of Karamania, 18 m. SE of Akshehr.

ARKLOW, a barony in the SE of co. Wicklow. Area 67,357 acres. Pop. 25,263. It gives the title of baron, by tenure, to the house of Ormonde.-Also a parish at the S extremity of this barony. Area 8,127 acres. Pop. 6,236. Houses 1,026. The Avoca drains it SE to the sea.—Also a seaport-town in this p., 8 m. NNE of Gorey, and 40 S by E of Dublin, on the river Avoca, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge of 19 arches. About 70 years ago this place was a mere fishing-hamlet; and the lower town, or the division nearest the sea, is still a wretched assemblage of hovels. The upper town, above the bridge, consists of respectable looking houses. About 50 small coasting vessels are employed in the trade of this town, besides 100 fishing-boats. The harbour is merely the mouth of the river within the bar, which has never above 4 or 5 ft. water upon it. The exports are copper-ore, corn, herrings, sheep, swine, and beer. An extensive shoal lies opposite the town, 5 m. from the coast. It is 10 m. long, and forms one of the most valuable oyster and herring fishinggrounds on the E coast of Ireland.
ARKONA. See ARCONA.
ARKSEY, a village in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. N of Doncaster. Pop. 266.
ARKWRIGHT, a town in Chautauque co., New York, U. S., 328 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 1,418.
ARL (GREAT and LITTLE), two rivers of Austria, which join the Salza near the town of St. John, in N lat. 47° 47'. Near the v. of Arl, on the Great Arl, copper and sulphur are mined.

ARLANT, or ARLANC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Saint d'Ambert. The cant. comprises 9 com. The commune and town are pleasantly situated on the Dolore, 45 m. SE of Clermont-Ferrand. Pop. 3,567. There are here manufactures of lace and ribands.
ARLANZA, a river of Spain, in Old Castile, which runs into the Arlanzon at Salduendo, near Burgos.
ARLANZON, a river of Old Castile, which, rising in the mountains of Occa, joins the Pisuerga, on the borders of Leon, after a course of 60 m.-Also a village on this river, 10 m. E by S of Burgos.

ARLBERG, a chain of mountains in German Austria, connected with the Rhætian Alps by those of the Grisons. It commences at the Adlersberg, or Arlberg,—a mountain in the Tyrol, which gives birth to a small rivulet of its own name, falling into the Rosanna, an affluent of the Inn. At the elevated point where the Lech and Inn originate, the chain bifurcates. One branch runs N with diminishing height towards Kemplen in Bavaria; and then W, in the south of Wirtemberg, and the grand-duchy of Baden, where, near the sources of the Danube, it meets the mountains of the Black ferest, which, as well as the Alps of Swabia, are a prolongation of this chain. The second branch accompanies the 1. bank of the Inn to its confluence with the Danube. The western versant of the first branch sends off to the Rhine the Ill, and to the lake of Constance, the Aach and the Argen. From the southern face of this branch, the Schussen descends to the same lake. All the rivers which descend from the northern sides of the two branches of the Arlberg run to the r. bank of the Danube. The principal streams are the Lech, the Iller, and the Isar. The southern versant of the second branch sends off no streams of any consequence to the Inn. The Arlberg-properly so called—is covered with pines. In 1786, a road was made across this range.

ARLE, a small town of Russia, in Livonia, near the source of the Woge.—Also a small town of Austria, in the electorate of Saltzburg, 10 m. SW of Radstadt.—Also a river in the duchy of Sleswick, which enters the sea 4 m. S of Bredstede.—Also a town in East Friesland, on the high road from Esens to Norden.
ARLEBOSC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, cant. of Saint-Felicien. Pop. 926.
ARLECDON, a parish of Cumberland. Area 5,700 acres. Pop. 568.
ARLEN, a town of Austria, in the Tyrol, situated on a mountain, 8 m. NW of Landeck.

ARLES, an ancient and still important town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, the cap. of an arrond, and cant, of the same name. It is situated in N lat. 43° 40′, and E long. 4° 37′, on the 1. bank of the Rhone, at the point of its division into the two great branches which form the large and marshy island of Carmagne. The main stream, called Grand Rhone, flows under the walls of the city, with a fall of 0.4 m. per kilometre, and a depth sometimes of 16 metres, in a channel of 800 metres broad. [Chaix.] The town is at the distance of about 25 m. from the embouchures of the river, and 38 m. from Marseilles. Pop. in 1846, 21,188. The more ancient part of the town consists of generally narrow, ill-built, and dilapidated streets. In its more modern districts, the houses are of better construction, the streets are spacious, and it is adorned with large and handsome squares; particularly the Place Royale, which forms a winter promenade, and is used also as a market-place and bull-ring. Its antiquities are numerous. It possesses an ancient obelisk, the largest monolith of granite in Europe, 55 ft. in height. It was discovered buried in the earth in a private garden, in 1339; and in 1675 was raised on a pedestal as a monument in honour of Louis XIV. Besides numerous architectural relics of its former ARL splendour-of which the most remarkable are the amphitheatre, converted in the middle age into a fortress-the ruins of two temples, of the palace of Constantine, a triumphal arch, and some other remains of Roman times, A. possesses a cathedral,a Gothic edifice of the 17th century,-three churches, of which that of Mont Majour forms an adjunct of the beautiful chapel and tower which belonged to the ancient abbey of the same name, a town-house, a library consisting of 15,000 volumes, a theatre, a museum, a chamber of commerce, an agricultural society, a communal college, and a hydrographical school. The environs are luxuriant and picturesque. The situation of A. on the Rhone and on the canal of the same name, by which it is connected with the Mediterranean, renders its commerce extremely active. The canal by which the difficult navigation of the mouths of the Rhone is avoided, extends along the l. bank of the Rhone, from A. to the port of Bouc, a distance of 27 m. It is 44 ft. wide at the bottom, and 68 ft. at the surface of the water, with a depth of 6 ft. The sides are raised 3 ft. above the highest level of the Rhone. It was opened in 1834 at a cost of £444,096. A. is the entrepot of Lyons and Marseilles; and its tonnage in 1837 amounted to 159,438 tons. Its trade consists principally in wine, grain, oil, soda, manna, fruits, sausages, horses, cattle, pigs, and wool. Grain markets are held twice a-week; and fairs four times in the year.—The earliest notice of A. occurs in Cæsar's Commentaries. It became a Roman colony about half a century before the Christian era, and was subsequently constituted by Constantine the metropolis of Gaul. Its decline commenced on the expulsion of the Visigoths. In 879 A. formed a portion of the kingdom of Provence; and in the 12th century it was constituted a republic, under the protection of the emperors of Germany. After its submission to Charles of Anjou in 1251, it shared the fate of the other parts of Provence. It was united to the crown of France in the reign of Louis XI. Previous to the revolution, A. was the see of an archbishopric, founded in the 4th cent., of which the bishoprics of Marseilles, St. Paul, Trois-Chateaux, Toulon, and Orange were suffragans. It has been the scene of several important councils, of which the first and most celebrated convened by Constantine, for the condemnation of the Donatists, occurred in 314, at which 600 bishops were assembled.—The arrond. of A. is divided into the 7 cantons of Arles, Chateau-Renard, Eyguières, Orgon, Saintes-Maries, Saint-Remy, and Tarascon. It has an area of 230,462 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 77,688; in 1846, 85,222.
ARLES, a village in Queen's co., 4 m. N of Carlow. Pop. 231.

ARLES-SUR-TECH, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, arrond. of Ceret. Pop. 2,166. Lead and iron are wrought here, and hot mineral springs are found in the neighbourhood. The cant. of A. comprises 10 com.

ARLESHEIM, a town of Switzerland, in the canton of Basle, on the r. bank of the Birs, 3 m. S of Basle, in N lat. 47° 29'. It is a well-built town, and is frequented as a watering place. The botanical garden is one of the finest in Switzerland. The castle of Birseck stands on a hill in its neighbourhood.
ARLET, a cape in the island of Martinique, in N lat. 14° 35', W long. 61° 7'.
ARLEUF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, arrond. of Chateau-Chinon. Pop. 2,442.
ARLEUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, 6 m. S of Douay. Pop. 1,744. It is a place of considerable manufacturing industry.—The cant. of A. comprises 15 communes.
ARLEY, a parish in the county of Warwick. ~ Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 265.—Also a river in the upper part of the great valley of Savoy, which loses its name in that of the Isére at Conflans.
ARLINGHAM, a village and parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. SE of Newnham. Area 2,300 acres. Pop. 793.

ARLINGTON, a township of Bennington co., Vermont, U. S., 12 m. N of Bennington, on the Green river. Here are extensive marble and limestone quarries, and a mineral spring. Pop. 1,038. -Also a p. in Devonshire, 5 m. NE of Barnstaple. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 206.—Also a p. in Sussex, 4 m. WSW of Hailsham. Area 4,790 acres. Pop. 686.

ARLON, a town of Belgium, in the grand-duchy of Luxemburg. It is seated on an eminence, surrounded by forests, 13 m. W of Luxemburg, near the source of the Semoy. Pop. 5,413. It has an active trade in corn and iron. A railway has been projected between this town and Namur. It is the Orolaunum, or Arlunum, of the early ages, and possesses a number of antiquities.
ARLSEY, a parish in Bedfordshire, 4 m. NW of Baldock. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 820.
ARLSTEIN. See ARNOLDSTEIN.
ARLY, a river of Italy, in Savoy, which joins the Isère near Conflans.
ARM (South), an extensive peninsula on the E side of the entrance to the river Derwent, in Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 43° 5', E long. 148° 30'.
ARM ISLAND, a small island at the entrance of the straits of Sunda, in S lat. 5° 45', E long. 106° 30'.
ARMA (Santiago de), a city of New Grenada, in South America, 150 m. NE of Popayan, on the Arma, a tributary to the Cauca. Its neighbourhood contains gold mines, and is fertile in grain and fruit.
ARMACOTTA, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 75 m. SE of Tanjore.

ARMADALE, a village in the parish of Sleat, on the S coast of the island of Skye. Agriculture and fishing are the occupations of the inhabitants.—Also a village in Lanarkshire, on the post-road midway between Edinburgh and Glasgow. Pop. 121.—Also a village in the parish of Farr, Sutherlandshire. The inhabitants are mostly engaged in the herring-fishery.

ARMAGH, an inland co. of Ulster, extending from Lough Neagh to the northern boundary of Leinster; bounded on the NW by Tyrone; on the N by Lough Neagh; on the E by Down; on the S by Louth; and on the W by Monaghan and Tyrone. Its form is a parallelogram of 24 m. by 11 m., with a considerable triangular protrusion at the NE corner, a smaller triangular protrusion in the SE, and a large curved expansion of 14 m. by 7 m. on the W side. It lies between lat. 54° 4' and 54° 30' N, and long. 6° 5' and 6° 45' W. Its greatest length is 25 m.; its greatest breadth 16 m.; its circumference is about 80 m.; and its area, according both to Dr. Beaufort's estimate and to Sir Charles Coote's statistical survey of the county, is upwards of 454 sq. m., or 290,786 English acres; but according to the Ordnance survey, 328,076 acres,—of which 265,243 acres are arable land, 35,117 acres are uncultivated, 8,996 acres are plantations, 778 acres are towns, and 17,942 are water. A broad band of country, bordering on Lough Neagh and the Blackwater, and expanding at one point as far S as to the environs of the city of Armagh, is flat, and extensively boggy. Much the larger part of the remaining surface is rolling ground. A district, comprehending about one-sixth of the whole area, and occupying nearly the whole of the southern border, is prevailingly upland, partly mountainous, and nearly all pastoral or waste.—The Fathom mountains, flanking Newry river, S of Newry town, have an alt. above sea-level of 820 ft.; the Foughal mountains, on the S border, 4 m. W of the former, have an alt. of 822 ft; the Slieve-Girkin or Newry mountains, WNW of Newry, have an alt. of 1,340 ft.; the Slieve-Gullion mountains, W of the Slieve-Girkin range, and separated from it by a narrow vale and a lake, have an alt. of 1,900 ft. The great inland loch on the N, shared by Armagh with four other counties, is separately described: see Neagh. A chain of small lakes occurs on the SW border, the chief of which are called Ross, St. Patrick's, and St. Peter's. But even the largest lakes in the county, which are Camlough between Slieve-Girkin and Slieve-Gullion, and Clay-Lough in the vicinity of Keady, are of very inferior magnitude compared to the fresh-water sheets of some neighbouring counties. The Newry canal runs along the eastern and the Ulster canal along the northern border. The river Bann, which continues the Newry navigation to Lough Neagh, has a NNW course within the county of about 11 m. The Blackwater flows NNE along the boundary with Tyrone, over a distance of 15 or 16 m. The other streams are the Callen, the Cushier, the Newtown-Hamilton, the Taree, and the Fane. The climate of this county is more mild, genial, dry, and healthy, than that of most other parts of Ulster; and the harvest, in consequence, is from 10 to 14 days earlier than in some neighbouring counties. SE winds are the most violent, moist, and prolonged. The medium temperature at the Armagh observatory is 49.5°.—Excepting in a small part of the Slieve-Gullion range, all the upland district in the SE of the county consists principally of granite. Some small spots in the neighbourhood of Newry are sienitic. A part of the Slieve-Gullion range, an exception to the granitic formation, consists of old red sandstone and sandstone conglomerate. A district comprehending about 64 or 70 sq. m., and lying N and W of the city of Armagh, but also sending a narrow belt along the western boundary to a point 5 m. S of the city, consists of carboniferous or mountain limestone. A district of 11 m. from E to W, and averagely about 44 m. from N to S, almost equally bisected by the river Bann, and lying around the towns of Lurgan and Portadown, consists of tabular trap. The district along Lough Neagh is of the tertiary formation. The rocks of the county are, in comparatively a very small degree, metalliferous or otherwise subservient to economical purposes. Lead, though traceable in various places, occurs in considerable quantity only around Keady, and even there has ceased to be mined. Ironstone is found rich in contents but not great in amount. Regulus of manganese and antimony have been seen in a very few and limited spots. Soapstone, coloured earths, a variety of ochres, and the clays in the tertiary formation, rank among the useful minerals. Marble of fair quality is quarried from the limestone beds in the vicinity of Armagh. The general soil is excellent, - occasionally prime, prevailingly fertile, and rarely incapable of improvement and cultivation. A sandy loam is probably of rarer occurrence than any other soil; and yet loam of other varieties predominates, and generally lies on an irretentive subsoil. Though much of the uplands is yet clothed with heath, ferns, and kindred herbage, very little is carpeted with moss, or of a character akin to turf-moor. Owing to the prevalence of the linen trade, and the great density of population, land is in such high demand that, even 40 years ago, a well-situated small estate currently brought in the market from 25 to 30 years' purchase; and at that period, the average rental of the cultivated districts was about 25s. per English acre. Farms, if the tiny parcels of land usually held by agricultural tenants may be dignified with that name, do not average, throughout the arable parts of the county, more than 5 acres. In 1841, the number of farms of from 1 acre to 5 acres each in extent, was 11,632; from 5 to 15 acres, 9,428; from 15 to 30 acres, 2,072; and upwards of 30 acres, 666. But this statement is exclusive of all out-districts. The extent of land under wheat in 1847 was 12,216 acres; oats 83,734 acres; barley 3,972 acres; beer 1,644 acres; and beans 1,133 acres: making a total under corn and beans of 103,021 acres. The land under potatoes was 9,652 acres; under turnips 10,661 acres; under flax 6,833 acres. The horned cattle of the county are light, seldom exceeding 3 or 4 cwt. The native sheep are coarse and ill-shapen, light in weight and coarse in wool. Goats are numerous and wild in the mountains; and may elsewhere be seen. In 1841, the total of horses and mules was 12,323; of cattle, 35,069; of sheep, 5,042; of pigs, 28,236; of poultry, 150,269; of asses, 263;—and the estimated value of the horses and mules was £98,584; of the asses, £263; of the cattle, £227,949; of the sheep, £5,546; of the pigs, £35,295; of the poultry, £3,756;—in all, £371,393. But these statements are exclusive of live stock to the value of £11,354 in the civic districts.—The total extent of woods, in 1841, exclusive of detached trees, was 8,996 acres; and of these 406 acres were oak, 19 ash, 10 elm, 17 beech, 212 firs, 5,549 mixed plantations, and 2,783 orchards. The number of detached trees was 406,075; and had they been amassed into woods, they would have occupied 2,538 acres. In Armagh and the other towns, brewing, chandling, soap-boiling, tanning, and the various arts of handicraft are practised for the supply of the local market. Woollen goods are manufactured throughout the county, yet only for domestic use, and not in sufficient quantity to supply more than probably one-tenth of the demand. But at once the staple trade, a general and prime means of support, and a pursuit associated to a large extent with that of agriculture, is, and long has been, the manufacture of linen. The fabrics produced are cambrics, lawns, diapers, damasks, chequers, and a great variety of plain linens. The weavers of the finest goods have both their residences and their markets in the north; and the weavers of the coarse fabrics are diffused chiefly along the western border. Hand-loom weaving still maintains predominance; and two or more looms are usually found in one cottage. Yet the introduction to Britain of machinery for spinning flax has exerted a strong influence on Armagh, and, to a certain extent, been locally copied.—The roads of the county are in general good and well kept. The railway commissioners projected a line of railway to connect Armagh and Dublin, and were anticipated by joint-stock companies or private parties in the projection and survey of other lines traversing or immediately affecting the county. A line from Belfast to Armagh, at the close of 1841, was open from Belfast to Lurgan. It is now open through. A line has been surveyed to deflect from the former at Portadown, run parallel to the Newry canal, and join at Newry a coast-line thence by Dundalk to the northern terminus of the Dublin and Drogheda railway. A line has been surveyed also to run northward from Armagh, and connect it along the western shore of Lough Neagh, and by way of Coleraine, with Portrush on the north coast of Antrim, the nearest port to the Scottish ports of Ardrossan and Greenock. So late as the year 1608, the only civil divisions of the co. of Armagh consisted of Ballyboes, each of which comprehended about 60 English acres. In the course of the next ten years the baronial division was introduced. It exhibits eight baronies, called Armagh, Lower Fews, Upper Fews, East O'Neilland, West O'Neilland, Lower Orior, Upper Orior, and Turaney. On the SE border also is a small part of * the lordship of Newry. In 1831, the civil parochial division, as followed in the population books, exhibited 18 parishes wholly in Armagh, 2 partly in Tyrone, 2 partly in Down, 1 partly in Louth, and 1 extra-parochial denomination. The present ecclesiastical parochial division differs widely from the political. The towns and principal villages are Armagh, Blackwatertown, Keady, and Charlemont, in the barony of Armagh; Belleek, Hamilton's-Bawn, and Market-hill, in Lower Fews; Crossmaglen and Newtown-Hamilton, in Upper Fews; Lurgan, in East O'Neilland; Portadown, Richhill, and Loughgall, in West O'Neilland; Acton, Tanderagee, and part of Poyntz-pass, in Lower Orior; Forkhill, Jonesborough, and part of Newry, in Upper Orior; and Middleton and Tynan in Turaney.-In 1824, according to Protestant returns, the number of schools in the county of Armagh was 332, of scholars 13,734, of male scholars 7,924, of female scholars 5,207, of scholars connected with the Established church 4,655, of scholars connected with Presbyterian bodies 2,864, of scholars connected with other bodies of Protestant dissenters 511, of scholars connected with the Roman Catholic community 5,281.—The annual amount of the valuation of the county under the 6 and 7 Will. IV., c. 84, is £242,005 0s. 7d.; and the expense of the valution was £4,874 16s. 8d., or 2.0125 per cent.—The county itself sends two members to parliament; and, including the borough of Armagh, three representatives for a total population of 232,393. In 1835, the constituency amounted to 3,480.—Pop., in 1790, as estimated by Dr. Beaufort, 120,000; in 1821, by census, 197,427; in 1831, 220,651; in 1841, 232,393. All the following statistics are those of 1841:-Males 113,892, females 118,501, families 43,175. Inhabited houses 41,297, uninhabited complete houses 2,186. Families employed chiefly in agriculture 24,260, in manufactures and trade 15,966, in other pursuits 2,949. Males at and above 5 years of age who could read and write, 38,803; who could read but not write, 22,727; who could neither read nor write, 37,375. Females at and above 5 years of age who could read and write, 19,319; who could read but not write, 35,180; who could neither read nor write, 49,508.

ARMAGH, a barony in the above county. Length, <math>14\frac{1}{4}</math> miles; breadth, <math>6\frac{3}{4}</math>; area, 47,866 acres. Pop., in 1831, 39,366; in 1841, 41,607. Houses 7,166.— Also a parish in the county of A. Length, 43 miles; breadth, 4\frac{1}{4}; area, 4,606 acres. Pop., in 1831, 10,518: in 1841, 12,654. Houses 1,883.

ARMAGH, a post, market, and ancient town, a royal borough, the cap. of the above county, and the ecclesiastical metropolis of Ireland, stands in the parish, barony, and county of Armagh, 15 m. NNW of Newry, 31 SW of Belfast, and 65 N by W of Dublin. The Callan and the Ballinahone rivers traverse the environs each at the distance of about 3 furlongs from the city, the former on the W, and the latter on the E; they form a confluence at a point half-a-mile from the city's northern extremity; and both are spanned by bridges, carrying across the numerous lines of thoroughfare. The body of the town is an oval ot about 500 yards by 330. The cathedral crowns Druimsailech, in the midst of an open area, in the very centre of the oval and of the city. Toward the area around it climb various streets, some obliquely, and some in the converging manner of radii. The present cathedral was built in 1675; renovated and greatly improved by Archbishop Robinson; and, a few years ago, repaired at a great cost. It is cruciform; and measures 119 ft. in the clear from end to end of the transepts, and 1831 ft. from east to west. or along the choir and the nave. The archiepiscopal residence, erected by Primate Robinson, is an elegant yet chaste and unostentatious structure. A college, alleged to have been founded here by St. Patrick, | was long one of the most celebrated seminaries in Europe. The present college, or grammar-school, or royal school, was founded by Primate Robinson. The observatory occupies an elevated site, 250 yards N of the college. Primate Robinson, the founder of the institution, endowed it with lands and a perpetual fund, which yield to the resident astronomer about £400 a-year. In the vicinity of the cathedral is a public library which presents a very handsome appearance; and contains spacious and neat accommodation for a collection of books, amounting to between 20,000 and 30,000 volumes. The Lunatic asylum for the counties of Armagh, Monaghan, Fermanagh, and Cavan, stands on the l. bank of the Ballynahone river, a furlong above its junction with the Callan, and was erected in 1824-5, at an expense of £20,000. Previous to the union, the borough sent two members to parliament; since the union, it sends one. Constituency, in 1841, 880 householders and 12 burgesses. Area of the town, 269 acres. Pop., in 1831, 9,470; in 1841, 10,245. Houses 1,493.—A city called Eamhain or Eamania—a name corrupted from Aem-huimue, 'the noble city'-is affirmed to have been built on or near the site of Armagh, by a Scottish prince, upwards of two centuries, or according to O'Connor, 353 years before the Christian era. This city claims to have been the capital of Ulster, and royal seat of the kings of the north. Previous to the conquest of Ireland by the English, and even in the various subsequent civil wars, A. suffered more of the horrors of fire, plunder, and sword, than probably any other Irish town of its size. "From time immemorial," to adopt the words of a periodical writer, "it has been the scene of many a deadly feud and bloody engagement, -in wars, waged at one time between the Irish chieftains themselves, -at others, between the Irish, Danes, and Ostmans,—and afterwards between the natives of the country and the invading armies of Scotland and England." By the act of 1833, the see of A. was so enlarged in territory as to comprehend that of Tuam, and, at the same time, so diminished in suffragans as to include, with the archbishop's own see, only 6 bishoprics. The present province extends over all Ulster, over by far the greater part of Connaught, and over a very considerable part of Leinster; and its 6 bishoprics are Armagh with Clogher, Meath still single, Derry with Raphoe, Down with Connor and Dromore, Kilmore with Elphin and Ardagh, and Tuam with Killalla and Achonry. A contentious struggle long maintained between Armagh and Dublin for priority, terminated some two or three centuries ago in the archbishop of Armagh being styled 'Primate of all Ireland and Metropolitan,' and he of Dublin 'Primate of Ireland and Metropolitan.' In 1833, the episcopal revenue was, gross, £17,669 16s. 7d.; nett, £14,494 0s. 3\frac{1}{4}d.; and was stated by the primate to be fairly capable of increase to the amount of £6,918 17s. 71d. The see lands are said to comprehend 104,000 acres. The diocese of Armagh comprehends nearly all the co. of Armagh and the co. of Louth, about a moiety of Tyrone, a considerable nook of Londonderry, and a very small part of Meath. Length, in statute measure, 75 m.; breadth, from 12½ to 32 m.; area, 809,289 acres. Pop., in 1831, 482,641.
ARMAGH, a township in Mifflin co., Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 1,468.
ARMAILLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, formerly appertaining to Anjou, 16 m. from Angers. Pop. 810.
ARMAMER, a town of Beira, in Portugal, 10 m. S of Pesquiero. Pop. 2,000.

ARMANA, a town of Syria, situated in a valley surrounded by rocks, 10 m. NW of Aleppo. It has a manufactory of glass. ARMANCE, a river of France, in the dep. of Yonne, joining the Armançon, near Florentine, after a course of 30 m.
ARMANÇON, a river of France, rising near Semur-en-Auxois, passing Semur, Ancy-le-Franc, Saint-Florentine, and Brinon l'Archeveque, and falling into the Yonne above Joigny.

ARMASAO, a small town of Brazil, at the extremity of a bay 4 leagues N of Santa Cruz, in S lat. 27° 5'. It is an important station for the whale fishery, which is said to be conducted with great skill by the establishment here. The harbour is commodious; but the number of whales caught on this coast has latterly decreased. The average number formerly carried to this port, amounted to 300 or 400 in a season.
ARMAVIR, a town of Armenia, on the Aras, 30 m. SW of Erivan, in N lat. 39° 50'.
ARMEL (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Rennes. Pop. 645.—Also a small fort in the dep. of Morbihan.

ARME'NIA. A country of Western Asia, in early times a part of the Persian empire founded by Cyrus, and subsequently divided into two sovereignties, - those of the Greater and the Lesser Armenia. That division, however, was merely political and arbitrary; and, as the country has no permanent natural boundaries, the term Armenia has been used by different writers with reference to portions of country of very different extent. Under the ancient Persian dynasty, Mount Ararat seems to have been the eastern, and the Northern Euphrates the western limit of Armenia; whilst the valley of the Araxes, belonging to the Saspirians, is classed as a distinct satrapy from Armenia by Herodotus in his account of the twenty satrapies of Darius Hystaspes. In the days of Xenophon, Armenia was divided into two satrapies,—the Eastern and the Western; the Centrites, or modern Khabur, forming the boundary line between the former and the Carduchians. Strabo extends Armenia as far E as the Greater and Lesser Media; with Adiabene on the SE, the Taurus and Mesopotamia on the S, and the Pontic nations, the mountain Paryadres, and the Euphrates, on the W. Ptolemy makes its boundaries much the same as those of Strabo. Pliny assigns to it a prodigious extent: giving it not less than 120 strategies, or provinces, 21 only of which are mentioned by Ptolemy. Moses of Chorene—a native Armenian, who wrote in the 5th century-furnishes a geographical account of the country in his Armenian history. But in this sketch he has given a most distorted account of the various divisions: and includes under the name of Armenia, the whole of Azerdbijan, Kurdistan, and Georgia. He assigns not less than 15 large provinces, and 187 smaller divisions, to Armenia; but only 11 of the former can be recognised as really belonging to it. Of the remaining four, Corzea belongs to Northern Kurdistan; Gugaria is manifestly Georgia, being a corruption of Kurgia or Gurgistaun; Taya is also clearly in Georgia; and Persarmenia is certainly the NW part of Azerdbijan. Of his 187 smaller divisions, not above 20 can be recognised in Greek and Roman writers as belonging to Armenia; and upwards of 90 of them can neither be identified in ancient nor in modern geography. At the present moment, Eastern or Persian Armenia is in possession of Russia; whilst the other parts belong to the Porte, and to such Kurdish beys as still recognise the supremacy of the Porte. Boundaries and Divisions.] Armenia, in its large and extensive acceptation, forms one great upland, or elevated plateau, extending from the sources of the Melas, Halys, Iris, Lycus, and Apsarus, on the W, to those of the Araxes, the Kur, and Arpa-Su, on the NE; and that of the Murad, or Southern Euphrates, on the E. From these opposite points, the plateau declines on one side towards the Euxine, and on the other towards the Caspian sea. The southern buttresses of this extensive upland are the Taurus, Antitaurus, Niphates, Masius, and the southern slope of the Kurdistan mountains. The northern boundaries are the Tchildir-Dagh, and the mountains of Pambaki and Capan-Dagh, which separate the basin of the Kur from that of the Aras. The eastern boundaries are a line drawn from the confluence of the Kur and Aras up the latter, to a small distance below Nakchivan; thence the line runs S towards the mountains which run between the lakes of Van and Urumiyah, and abut on the Tigris a little below Zako, thus forming the limit on the side of Assyria and Azerdbijan. Thence it goes up the Tigris to the gorge or narrow pass formed by the convergence of the Niphates and Masius; thence along the latter to Mount Taurus, as far as the Euphrates, separating it from Mesopotamia, and including in its bosom the large valley of Diarbekir, and the western sources of the Tigris. It then turns N, separating the basin of the Euphrates from the rivers of Cappadocia and Pontus, as far as the source of the Apsarus; whence it runs along the lofty chain which separates that valley from the coast of the Black sea, commonly called the Colchian range, and which joins the western angle of a lateral branch of the Caucasus, enclosing the western source of the Kur. This forms the western limit of Armenia, separating it from Guriel, or Southern Colchis. The region thus defined, is politically divided into Turkish, Russian, and Kur- DISH ARMENIA. The first—which is by far the largest portion-comprehends the whole of the western part of the plateau reaching from the Apsarus and the western source of the Kur, to the source of the Arpa- Su, which, to the N of the Aras, now forms the limit on the side of Russian, as it heretofore did of Persian Armenia. On the S of the Aras it extends to the Tigris and Mount Ararat or Masius; on the SE to the range of Zako; on the E it follows the range of mountains which run between the lakes of Urumiyah and Van, from SE to NW; thence it proceeds to the SE of Bayazid across the Aras; thence NW to the confluence of the Aras and Arpa-Su; and thence N to the source of the latter in the Pambakhi range. The old Turkish and Persian frontiers constitute its eastern boundary. It comprehends the pashaliks of Akhalzikhe, now partly ceded to Russia; of Erzerum, Kars, Bayazid, Van, Diarbekir, and Mush.—Russian Armenia comprehends the NE part of Armenia to the E of the Arpa-Su, and to the N of the Aras, composed of the khanates of Erivan and Nakchivan .- Kurdish Armenia is situated to the S and SW of the lake of Van; and is subject to a number of Kurdish beys, as those of Biblis, Julamerik, and others. Physical features. A. is not merely a lofty plateau surrounded by mountains, but is itself intersected in various directions by numerous ranges and ridges, of which it is merely the base. Not fewer than five ranges intersect the route from Trebisond to Erzerum, running parallel to each other. Of these, the Cope-Dagh—the Capotes of the ancients—is the highest and most eastern. The lofty range of the Tchildir-Dagh, the Pambakhi, and the Capan-Dagh, cover Armenia on the N, separating the basin of the Kur from that of the Aras. The Saganlu-Dagh separates the course of the Kars from the plain of the Aras. The Aleghez forms the eastern boundary of the valley of Shuragil. The Ala, or Abi-Dagh, separates the basin of the Aras from that of the Murad. About 100 m. E of Erzerum this range divides into two branches, and the space thus enclosed forms the valley of the Murad; the two branches reunite beyond its source near Bayazid. The Kebban-Dagh runs parallel to the course of the Northern Euphrates. The Bingheul-Dagh, or Pinkiol, separates the Lycus from the Kara-Su. The Tegh-Dagh, also called the Bingheul-Dagh, separates the valley of the Aras from the source of the river of Khanus. The Hamur-Dagh separates the course of the Khanus from the Murad. Finally, the Nemrud-Dagh, or ancient Niphates, or Snowy mountain, separates the basin of the Murad from that of the Tigris; and sweeping round to the SE, encloses in its bosom the large lake and district of Van, separating them from the basin of the lake of Urumiyah. At this point it is called Caspius, or the Snowy mountain, by Eratosthenes and Ptolemy,—a term of identical signification with that of Niphates. From the Nemrud-Dagh, a lateral range running S, and to the W of the Van lake, joins the Hatarash, another stupendous range, which, coming from the SE, from the range of Mush, runs along the S side of the Van lake, as far E as the mountains of Giroos and Sahund, which separate Azerdbijan from Armenia. The whole range, with its diverging branches, justly deserves the epithet of Niphates, or Snowy. In respect of magnitude, it is the largest of the Armenian ranges, extending over seven degrees of long., from the Euphrates on the E, to the boundary of Azerdbijan; and varying from 80 to 120 m. in breadth, in direct distance between the Tigris and the Murad- Shai. Besides these ranges are the Taurus and its two branches enclosing the valley of Diarbekir; the ridge of Zako abutting on the Tigris, and the range of Al-Judi skirting its left bank. Rivers and Lakes.] The Euphrates, Tigris, Kur, Araxes, and Apsarus, or Chorok, are the chief rivers of A., the rest being merely tributaries to one or other of these streams. The lakes are those of Colchos, or Gurgik, in the recesses of the Taurus; the large lake of Van surrounded by mountains; that of Erivan, or the Sevan lake; the lake of Balaghez, at the source of the Palakat-Shai, a branch of the Arpa- Su; the Erzen lake, between the Batman-Su and the Susan branch of the Tigris, the Thospilis lake of the ancients. The lakes of Shellu and Nazuk have been but lately discovered. The former, on the side of the Nemrud-Dagh, near the Murad,—a noble sheet of fresh water, a day's journey in circumference; the latter is in the very heart of that range. Climate.] Such a high plateau as Armenia, crowned with ranges of lofty mountains, must have a comparatively cold elimate. Indeed the cold is so great that all kinds of fruits are more backward here than in most northern countries. Many of the mountains are covered with permanent snow; and most of them have it ten months annually. Even in the middle of July ice is often found about the springs before sunrise, though it be very hot during the day; and snow sometimes falls at Erzerum in June and July. Schulz found the hills between Trebisond and Erzerum covered with deep snow in the month of June. Lucullus, the Roman general, was surprised to find the whole country covered with snow, and most of the rivers frozen, during the autumnal equinox. Antony, in his retreat from Azerdbijan through A., in the winter-season, lost 8,000 men, who perished in the snow and from the excessive cold. The same fate befel the army of Alexander Severus; and it appears wonderful how the famous 10,000 Greeks, under Xenophon, in the full rigour of an Armenian winter, and in daily want of provisions, escaped death from cold and hunger. In Kinneir's opinion, no military campaign could last beyond four or five months in Western A., on account of the severity of the season and the impracticability of the roads. The lower part of the ~ district of Ispira is the hottest in all A. In the valley of Diarbekir the climate is mild, and very hot in summer; and the temperature gradually improves in the route from Erzerum to Van, and from thence to the banks of the Upper Tigris. Soil and Produce.] From what has been stated of the high elevation and mountainous nature of this region, and its long winters and rigid climate, the soil and produce of Armenia cannot be expected to equal those of Asia Minor. Between Erzerum and the upper course of the Acampsis the climate is severe, the soil sterile, and the produce scanty; no trees are seen; and the winters are so severe at Baiabut, that all communication betwixt it and the circumjacent villages is annually cut off by the snow for at least four months. The grass, however, is rich; and the mountains, though bare of wood, are clothed with a variety of fragrant plants, as thyme, balm, and other aromatics. In the lower valley of the Acampsis, the soil is excellent, well-watered, and very productive of fruits and grain. In the immense plain of Erzerum not a tree is to be seen, yet forage is abundant in spring and summer, and considerable quantities of grain are produced in the vicinity. Horses and cattle are also abundant and cheap. All graminivorous animals, as cows, sheep, and horses, thrive well in A., on account of the fine grass on the mountainslopes, and in the valleys, which are remarkably wellwatered. In the vicinity of Van the winter lasts nine months, leaving only three months for seed-time and harvest; yet the crops of corn are abundant. Walnut and apple-trees exist here; and in the vicinity of Aklat apples of a pound weight are obtained. As we descend the Euphrates, the climate improves, and vines and olives occur. In the extensive valley of Diarbekir the soil is excellent, producing two crops of wheat and barley annually. The hills between Merdin and Diarbekir are interspersed with vineyards; and the bottoms of the valleys with orchards and gardens of apricots, peaches, mulberries, and walnuts. Marco Polo notices the excellence of the cotton grown in this region.—In ancient times A., it is said, possessed mines of gold; but these—if ever they existed—have now disappeared, and the chief mineral wealth of the country consists in iron and copper. In the Lesser A., W of the Euphrates, and in the mountainous region bordering on Pontus and Cappadocia, iron we know was very abundant in ancient times. The most productive mines at present known to exist in A. are those of Argana and Kebban. The Argana mines are situated on the southern slope of the Antitaurus, which looks down on the great valley of Diarbekir, 50 m. NW of the city whence the valley takes its name. The other mine is in the very heart of the Kebban range, 70 m. N of Argan. Cities. The principal modern cities of Armenia are Erzerum and Kars. Of the cities of ancient Armenia, some ruins are yet to be seen, which display a good style in architecture; e.g. the ruins of the old capital, Ani, in N lat. 40° 32′, E long. 43° 36′ [Porter], which was destroyed in 1319 by an earthquake; and those of the ancient city Armavir, which, during 1,800 years, was the residence of the kings of A. After Armavir, Artaxarta on the Araxes, built in the time of the Seleucidæ, became the cap., but sank into decay before the end of the 8th cent. Population. The present inhabitants of this country are a mixture of Armenians, Kurds, Turks, and Eelauts; but we have no means of ascertaining the relative proportions of each of these races. The first class are the aborigines of the country; the third class are the Osmanli Turks; the other two classes belong to the nomadic or wandering tribes. As two tribes of Turcomans, called Kara-Kyonlu and Ak-Kyonlu, successively conquered A., it has been usual to call アナリン ハナバス・バクラ さ ヘインタイピョン ト ハトガルルルクタイク・ファント・ファン・バン・バン this country Turcomania; and its inhabitants, Turcomans. But Tavernier-who, though an illiterate, was still an intelligent and observing traveller-very properly condemns this practice, since the majority of the population are still Armenians, and the Turcomans only wandering tribes, who are in fact as numerous in Anatolia and Karamania as in Armenia, and are likewise found scattered through Mesopotamia, Syria, the whole of Persia, and the tract on the E of the Caspian sea. Inordinate love of gain seems to be a distinguishing feature of the modern Armenians. They are a patient, frugal, industrious race, however; and whether in their own country, or in a foreign abode, generally live in large families, under the patriarchal rule of the eldest member, and in a state of happy concord. Language and Religion.] In their own language the Armenians are called Hai-Kani from King Haikh, grandson of Japhet, as Moses of Chorene informs us. But their early history is wrapt up in such deep obscurity that nothing can be made of it. Strabo tells us that there was a great similarity in manners and language between the Syrians and Armenians: and Bochart, building on this assertion, maintains that the Syrians and Armenians had the same language and alphabet; but this notion has been completely overthrown by modern philologists, who find no affinity in its syntax to the Shemitic languages, but some to the Japhetian. It does not appear that the Armenians had an alphabet till the time of Mesrob, who flourished in the 5th cent., and was the inventor of their present alphabet. Into this Armenian written language the Bible was translated; and in this ecclesiastical alphabet all their books are still written, and their religious services performed. The conversion of the Armenians, by Gregory, took place in the reign of Tiridates, the best and greatest of their monarchs of the Arsacian line, and cotemporary with Constantine the Great. Until the invention of the ecclesiastical alphabet—if it may be so denominated—the Western Armenians used the Greek language and characters in their religious services; while, in the eastern districts, the Syriac was used for the same purpose, as the Persians had prohibited the use of the Greek language in that region of Armenia. It may indeed be asked, if the Armenians had a written language peculiar to themselves, how comes it to pass that they were obliged to make use of two alphabets, the Greek and the Syriac, for the purpose of conveying and receiving religious instruction? Had they a varied form of the cuneiform character, used anciently in Assyria, Media, and Persia? And if so, was it found to be an inconvenient vehicle for recording or communicating knowledge? We learn from Polyænus that the Armenians used the Syriac characters; for he tells us that Eumenus produced a letter from Orontes, satrap of Armenia, written in Syriac characters. Now, this was 300 years before Christ, and we find the same language used seven centuries afterwards in Eastern A. The subject is obscure, and we have no means of elucidating it. But it is certain that the modern Armenians have two languages, the sacred and the vulgar. To be well-versed in the former is an indispensable requisite for an Armenian vertabet, or theological doctor. The vulgar or secular Armenian dialect is spoken by Armenian merchants and colonists in Russia, Turkey, Arabia, Persia, Turkestan, India, Indo-China, and China; and has preserved its purity to a remarkable extent in all these regions. The Armenians were considered as belonging to the orthodox church till the middle of the 6th cent., when the missionaries of Julian of Halicarnassus brought them over to the Eutychian heresy, in which they have ever since continued. They have no communion with the other Monophysite sects, whether in Asia or Africa; the Nestorians they abhor; and they detest the errors and idolatry of the Greek church as sincerely as they formerly did the Magian system of their Persian conquerors of the Sassanian dynasty, or as they still do, the worship of the false prophet. The popes have, at different times, when they requested protection against the Mahommedans, attempted to gain the Armenians over to the Catholic faith, but have not been able to unite them permanently and generally with the Roman church. In Italy, Poland, Gallicia, in the Russian government Ekaterinoslav, and in Marseilles, there are United Armenians who acknowledge the spiritual supremacy of the Pope, and agree in their doctrines with the Catholics, but retain their peculiar ceremonies and discipline. The case is the same with the United Armenian monasteries upon Mount Lebanon in Syria. At the Persian invasion, in the beginning of the 17th cent., many of them were obliged to become Mahommedans; but the far greater part are yet Monophysites, and have remained falthful to their old religion and worship. In general, they surpass all the kindred Monophysitic sects in information; they allow the people to read the Bible; study the theological, historical, and mathematical sciences; possess a respectable national literature, and, at Etzmiadzin, have a printing office, which produces splendid copies of the Bible. In the year 1700, Mechitar Pedrosian, a Catholic Armenian, founded a new monastery in the Moslem capital, of which he was himself appointed abbot. Being persecuted by the adverse, or old Oriental sect, he fled with his monks to the Morea, then subject to Venice, and established his monastery at Modon. The Morea reverted to the Ottoman sceptre, and in 1717 the worthy abbot transferred his monastery and academy to Venice, where, upon the island of San Lazarro, it has ever since remained and prospered. In honour of its founder it is called Mechitarist. Abbot Mechitar, during the remainder of his life, taking advantage of a situation which enabled him to combine the knowledge of Europe with that of his native land, diligently and successfully exerted himself to render his monastic college the principal seat of Armenian erudition and education, and thither all such of his countrymen as desire a superior degree of cultivation for their offspring still send their sons for instruction. The best Armenian printing-press extant is the Mechitarist, from which press issues a newspaper; and neither the monks nor their superiors neglect any of the opportunities for improvement that they possess. Their chief literary occupations are, indeed, more useful to their less enlightened countrymen, than interesting to strangers, namely, translating into Armenian the classic works of France, Italy, England, and Germany. The Armenians have three patriarchs, those of Etzmiadzin, Aghtamar, and Sis. The former of these spiritual personages has 47 archbishops in his diocese-which extends over all the Greater Armenia-each of whom claims the homage of four or five suffragan bishops consecrated by his hand; but the greater part are merely titular prelates who only dignify by their occasional presence and service the patriarchal court, and as soon as they have performed the liturgy, return to cultivate their gardens. The revenues of this patriarch are calculated at 600,000 crowns; but this enormous revenue is insufficient to supply the incessant demands of charity and tribute, though this last is in all probability abolished since the Russian conquest. The second patriarch, at Aghtamar, on the lake of the Van, has only 8 or 9 bishops under him. The third patriarch, at Sis, has 12 archbishops and 20,000 families in his diocese extending throughout Cappadocia, Cilicia, Cyprus, and Syria, Besides these, the Armenians have three other patriarchs at Constantinople, Jerusalem, and Kaminieck, in Russian Poland. The whole number of Armenian Christians was estimated at 1,700,000 about 150 years since, and it has not probably diminished. History.] Of the ancient history of this country little is known. It appears to have been subjected, in turn, by the Assyrians, Medes, Persians, and Macedonians. After the death of Alexander, it became part of the kingdom of Syria, and so remained till the overthrow of Antiochus the Great, when it fell into the hands of different rulers, and was divided into A. Major and Minor. A. Major was exposed to many attacks. The Romans and Parthians disputed a long time the right of giving a successor to the throne; it was governed at one period by Parthian princes, at another, by those whom the Romans favoured, until Trajan made it a Roman province. A afterwards recovered its independence, and was under the rule of its own kings. Sapor, king of Persia, attempted its subjugation in vain; and it remained free until 650, when it was conquered by the Arabians. After this, A. several times changed its masters, among whom were Jengis-Khan and Tamerlane. In 1552, Selim II. wrested it from the Persians, and the greater part has since remained under Turkish dominion. A. Minor has also had several rulers, among whom Mithridates was first distinguished. From him Pompey took the kingdom, and gave it to Dejotarus. On the decline of the Roman empire in the East, it was conquered by the Persians; and, in 950, fell into the hands of the Arabians; since which time it has shared the same fate as A. Major, and was made, in 1514, a Turkish province by Selim I. In 1828, the whole of Persian A., to the N of the Araxes, with some districts to the S of that stream, were ceded to Russia; and there seems much probability that the Turkish part will soon share the same fate. Venice, 1841.

ARMENIERSTADT, or SZAMOS UJVAR, a wellbuilt town of Transylvania, in the co. of Solnok, on the Little Samosch, built by an Armenian colony in 1726. It is protected by a strong castle; and has various manufactures on a small scale. Its principal trade is in cattle. Pop. 3,400.

ARMENRUHE, a village of Silesia, in the principality of Leignitz, circle of Goldberg. Pop. 700. ARMENT-anciently Hermontis-a village on the Nile, 16 m. N of Esneh. The ruins of a temple, and other antiquities, denote its former importance.
ARMENTEQUI, a small town of Spain, in Biscay, near Vittoria. It was once a city and an archbishop's see.

ARMENTIERES, a canton, commune, and small town of France, in the dep. of Nord. The cant. ARM comprises 7 com. The com. and town had a pop. of 6,338 in 1844. The town, 8 m. NW of Lisle, is beautifully situated on the Lys, which passes through it, and forms a harbour for small vessels. It is a place of considerable trade, and has manufactures of linen, cotton, and other stuffs, bleach-works, brandydistilleries, potteries, and tanneries. Its fortifications have been dismantled.
ARMENTO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 20 m. SE of Potenza, on the declivity of a rock surrounded by precipices. Pop. 2,800.
ARMES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, cant. of Clamecy. Pop. 481.
ARMET, a town of the island of Nassa-Laut, one of the Moluccas, in N lat. 3° 42', E long. 128° 45'.
ARMI (CAPO DELL'), a cape of Naples, on the S coast of Calabria Ultra 2da, in N lat. 37° 57', E long. 15° 40'.
ARMILLAS, a town of Spain, in Aragon, 44 m. S of Saragossa.
ARMIN, a chapelry in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. E of Snaith. Area 3,660 acres. Pop. 593.
ARMINGFORD, a hundred of Cambridgeshire, on the borders of Hertford, comprising 14 parishes. Area 28,771 acres. Pop. in 1831, 7,539; in 1841, 8,698.
ARMINGHALL, a parish of Norfolkshire, in Henstead hund. Area 900 acres. Pop. 79.
ARMIRA, a river of Darien, falling into the ocean near Cape Tiburon.—Also a port of Calabria, on the W side of the gulf of Tarento, in N lat. 39° 10'.
ARMITAGE, a parish in Staffordshire, 2 m. SE from Rudgeley. Area 1,950 acres. Pop. 987. A tunnel of the Grand Trunk canal passes through a hill in this parish.

ARMLEY, a chapelry and village in Yorkshire, on the river Aire, and the Liverpool canal, in the parish and 2 m. S of Leeds. Area of chapelry 1,040 acres. Pop. 5,676. Extensive woollen manufactures are carried on here.
ARMONA, a town of Greece, in the island of Negroponte, 22 m. SE of Negroponte.
ARMOND (St.), a town of British North America, at the N end of Lake Champlain.
ARMONTABO, a river in French Guayana, which falls into the Oyapock, after a winding course of 117 m.
ARMOY, a parish in co. Antrim. Area 665 acres. Pop. 2,766.
ARMSCOTT, a hamlet in Worcestershire, near Shipston-on-Stour. Pop. 139. With Newbold it forms a distinct parish.
ARMSDALE-HEAD, a cape on the N coast of Scotland, in N lat. 58° 36', W long. 3° 56'.
ARMSEN, a parish of Hanover, containing six villages, in the principality and 7 m. SE of Verden.
ARMSHEIM, a neat village in Hesse-Darmstadt, formerly in the palatinate of the Rhine, 4 m. N of Abzey. Pop. 900.
ARMSTON, a hamlet in Polebrook parish, Northamptonshire. Pop. 26.
ARMSTORF, a market-town of Bavaria, in the district of Landshut, on the Kalbach. There are two fine old castles here.

ARMSTRONG, a co. in Pennsylvania, U. S.; bounded on the N by Venango co.; E by Jefferson co.; SW by Westmoreland; and W by Butler co. Area 575 sq. m. Pop. 28,365. The chief town is Kittanning, 214 m. NW of Harrisburg. - Also a town in Indiana co., Pennsylvania, on Crooked creek. Pop. 1,054.

ARMSTRONG'S CHANNEL, in Van Diemen's Land, between Cape Barren island and Clarke island, in N lat. 40° 28', E long. 148° 14'. It is about 10 m. long in an E and W direction, and from 3 m. to 4 m. wide. The tide runs from 3 to 4 m. an hour in the deep and narrow parts.
ARMTHORPE, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, near Doncaster. Area 2,810 acres. Pop. 449.
ARMUNA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. SE of Guadalajara, on the Tajuna.
ARMUYDEN. See ARNEMUYDEN.
ARMYRO, a town of Turkey in Europe, on a bay of the same name, on the W side of the gulf of Volo, in N lat. 39° 13'.—Also a town of Greece, in Acarnania, on the E coast of the gulf of Arta.

ARNA, the cap. of a district in the Desert between Fezzan and Bornu, inhabited by the Tibbu tribe, 250 m. SE of Murzuk, in N lat. 24° 30'.—Also a town of Kharasm, in Tartary, 200 m. N of Urkonje.—Also a seaport town on the E coast of Andros, in the Archipelago. It is the seat of a Roman and Greek bishop.
ARNAC-LA-POSTE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, 18 m. E by N of Bellac. Pop. 1,792.

ARNAC-POMPADOUR, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, 3 m. SE of Lubersac. A noble castle built here in 1026, by Guy de Latour, was given by Louis XV. to Madame d'Etoile. Pop. 1,196.
ARNACH, a village of Swabia, in the co. of Wolfegg, now belonging to Wirtemberg. Pop. 900.
ARNADELA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, 8 m. S of Villa Franca, in N lat. 42° 41'.
ARNAO, a coal-mining village, on the coast of Gallicia in Spain, 3 m. from Arviles. The seam of coal here runs under the sea, and is 40 ft. thick.
ARNARA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 5 m. SE from Frosignone. Pop. 1,500.
ARNARFELL, a prodigious ice-mountain or range of mountains, near the centre of Iceland, stretching from the Kerlingar-fialla volcanoes, in a NE direction, for upwards of 50 m., and then E 30 m.
ARNAS, a town of Angermannland, in Sweden, 52 m. NE of Hernösand.—Also a town of Prussia, in the district of Oberland, 4 m. SW of Osterode.— Also a town in the island of Cuba, in N lat. 20° 38'.

ARNAU, a small town of Bohemia, in the circle of Bitschow, on the l. bank of the Elbe, 9 m. N of Koningsgratz. Pop. 2,300. A considerable quantity of linen is manufactured here. It has also a large bleach-field, dye-houses, and copper-works.
ARNAUD-BELLIGRAD, a town of Albania, 40 m. NE of Vallona.
ARNAUTKOI, a village near Constantinople.
ARNAVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Thiaucourt. Pop. 872.

ARNAY-LE-DUC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or. The cant. comprises 20 com. The com. and town have a pop. of 2,563, and carry on a good trade in vinegar, oil, grain, hemp, and cattle.
ARNCLIFFE, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 35,860 acres. Pop. 834.
ARNCLIFFE-INGLEBY, a township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, in Langbaurgh liberty. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 329.
ARNCOTT, a village and chapelry in Oxfordshire, in Ambroseden parish. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 331.
ARNDAL. See ARENDAL.
ARNDORF, two small towns of Bavaria, one 4 m. E of Kemnat, and the other 18 m. SE of Bayreuth.
ARNE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, in N lat. 40° 20'.—Also a parish in Dorsetshire, on the coast. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. 168.

ARNEBURG, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, on the Elbe, between Angermund and Werben, 3 m. distant from each. The ruins of an old castle here give the title of baron of Arneburg to the family of Waldeck. The town contains several cloth-manufactories, and has some trade in corn. Pop. 1,475.
ARNECK, a village and castle of Wirtemberg, in ARN the circle of the Danube, on the Blau. It was formerly in the territory of the city of Ulm.

ARNEDILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Burgos, about 12 miles SSE from Calahorra, on the Cidacos river, an affluent of the Ebro. It is celebrated for its baths of an average heat of 32° R. Their principal ingredient is muriate of soda. Excellent wine is produced in the vicinity.
ARNEE, a town of Hindostan, 14 m. S of Arcot, in N lat. 12° 39', E long. 79° 23'. It was taken by the British in 1754.
ARNEIRA, a town of Portugal, on the sea-coast, 20 m. S of the mouth of the Tagus, in N lat. 38° 51'.
ARNELLAS, a village in the prov. of Beira, in Portugal, 38 m. W of Lamego, on the Douro. It has a considerable trade in wine and salt.
ARNEKE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Cassel. Pop. 1,493.
